architecture.summary: RESOLVE is a neuro-vector symbolic architecture that combines object-level features with relational representations in high-dimensional space. Information flows as follows: input objects are encoded to low-dimensional embeddings, then mapped to high-dimensional space via an HD-Encoder (single-dimension convolution with learnable HD-Basis vectors). A novel HD-Attention mechanism computes relational scores using bipolar cosine similarity in this high-dimensional space. The attention weights mix HD-encoded objects via bundling (summation). Learnable symbolic vectors representing relations are also mapped to HD space and bound (Hadamard product/element-wise multiplication) with the mixed object vectors, allowing relational and object-level information to coexist with minimal interference. The resulting high-dimensional vectors are then projected back to low-dimensional space via a learnable linear layer and passed to attentional decoders.
trained_components: HD-Attention mechanism (bipolar projections and cosine similarity scoring)

biblio.title: RESOLVE: Relational Reasoning with Symbolic and Object-Level Features Using Vector Symbolic Processing

claims.statement: RESOLVE is the first to propose a strategy for addressing the relational bottleneck problem using a vector symbolic architecture, capturing relational representations of input objects in a hyperdimensional vector space while maintaining object-level information in the same representation structure with minimized interference

claims.dataset: Order relations (64 random objects, 4096 possible pairs)
claims.statement: On the order relations task (learning asymmetric relations from limited examples), RESOLVE achieves over 90% accuracy with just 210 training samples, performing better than the second best model and better than Abstractor
On the SET task with pre-processed objects, RESOLVE outperforms all baselines up to x better than the second best model and x better than the Abstractor
On the SET task with low-processed objects, RESOLVE outperforms state of the art with more than 92% accuracy using just 600 training samples

On MNIST-Math (extracting non-linear weighted subtraction rule from digit image pairs), RESOLVE outperforms other baselines with better accuracy than the transformer and better accuracy than the Abstractor
On 6-element sequence sorting (purely relational sequence-to-sequence task), RESOLVE achieves better accuracy than baselines (x to x better than Relational-Abstractor) on element-wise accuracy metric

On mathematical reasoning (partially-relational sequence-to-sequence: Numbers_place_value, Comparison_pair, Comparison_closest), RESOLVE achieves 41.86% overall accuracy (average across three task types and 100–10k training set sizes), outperforming state-of-the-art, and achieves higher accuracy with small training sets

The HD-Attention mechanism has better computational performance compared to self-attention with higher memory bandwidth utilization due to use of bipolar HD representation and operations such as bundling and binding
